interactions.summary: Jiang argues that ancient gods and spirits were truthful interpretations of right-brain contact with the universe, using Jaynes, ancient burials, ancestor skulls, plant consciousness, scientific inspiration, everyday uncanny experiences, and Homer's invocation of the Muse.
Jiang asks a series of rhetorical questions about telepathy, dreams, possession, inner conversations, and guardian angels to make students notice latent belief in a spirit world.
Jiang reads from the Fagles translation of the Iliad's opening invocation to the Muse, then interprets it as channeling rather than authorial invention.

claims.claim: For most of human history, humans interpreted right-brain contact with the universe as gods and spirits; this may be non-factual but truthful because it helps people understand themselves and reality.
claims.temporal_scope: General account of premodern consciousness in this lecture.

claims.claim: Modern materialism shuts down the right hemisphere and therefore makes contemporary people less creative than the Greeks.
claims.temporal_scope: Modernity diagnosis contrasted with ancient Greece.

claims.claim: Ancient burials and ancestor worship are evidence that the dead were experienced as still present and able to speak, advise, or demand accommodation.
claims.temporal_scope: Prehistoric and ancient religious practice as used in Jiang's model.

claims.claim: Jiang extends consciousness to the whole universe: plants, animals, humans, and DNA-based life form a communicative network that can send insight to receptive minds.
claims.temporal_scope: Speculative metaphysical model stated in the lecture.

claims.claim: Homer's invocation to the Muse means he is channeling information from the gods rather than merely creating it as an isolated author.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's interpretation of the Iliad's opening.

signature_moments.moment: Gods are not factual, but they may be truthful.
signature_moments.source_phrase: even though it's not factual it is truthful
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This becomes Jiang's key distinction for defending myth against modern literalism.

signature_moments.moment: Scientific breakthroughs arrive as if a spirit is whispering secrets to scientists.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a spirit is telling the secrets of the universe to the scientists
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It lets Jiang fold modern science back into the same inspired-consciousness model as ancient poetry.

signature_moments.moment: Homer is not creating but channeling.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It reframes authorship as reception and makes poetry a bridge between worlds.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's distinction for myths, gods, and spiritual explanations that may not describe literal events but reveal psychological or metaphysical structure.
Neolithic ancestor practice that Jiang uses as evidence for living contact with the dead and spirit-world wisdom.
The divine source Homer invokes; Jiang reads the Muse as evidence of channeling rather than solitary composition.

chronology_notes.note: By November 2025, Jiang is explicitly connecting Homer, Jaynes, Kant/Hegel, plant consciousness, and modern science into one anti-materialist explanation of Greek wisdom.

uncertainty_notes.note: The cited 'constant serpent' appears to be Jeremy Narby's The Cosmic Serpent; ASR likely misheard the title and author.
